text: My name is Thomas Clancy. I'm the associate chief of surgical oncology at Brigham and Women's Hospital in Boston and a cancer surgeon at Brigham and Women's in Danube Harbor.

Today we're going to be talking about a patient who underwent a robotic liver resection for colorectal cancer spread to the liver. The patient had been treated for colorectal cancer and on routine surveillance was found to have an area of spread or metastasis to the right lobe of the liver. Surgical resection was offered as part of her treatment course.
Based on her favorable anatomy, we felt that a minimally invasive approach would be warranted. This patient was felt to be an ideal candidate for robotic hepatectomy.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to mobilize the liver by separating it from its attachments, carefully dissecting through areas of scar tissue. The robotic tools provide enhanced articulation, allowing precise movements to detach the liver from the posterior structures.

The surgeon uses an intraoperative ultrasound device to visualize the tumor within the liver, scanning through the liver tissue to ensure no other lesions are present. The ultrasound image is displayed on the screen for precise examination.

The surgeon uses an ultrasound to identify the tumor area in the liver, then scores and coagulates a line on the liver surface with a harmonic scalpel. This marks the planned resection area, ensuring a margin of healthy liver tissue is maintained around the tumor.

The surgeon controls robotic instruments to perform coagulation through the liver, ensuring a hemostatic approach to minimize blood loss. The assistant uses a suction aspirator to manage fluids, while gentle retraction is applied to the liver. Instruments are prepared to grasp vessels in case of bleeding.

In this case, one advantage of the robotic technique is the three-dimensional visualization One thing not appreciated on the video is the fact that the surgeon is able to appreciate true three-dimensional visualization on the camera due to the fact that essentially two cameras are used.

The surgeon rotates the liver upward to access and dissect along its posterior border. Metal clips are visible on the structures where the gallbladder was previously attached, indicating its removal to clear the resection margin. The dissection continues through the liver to facilitate specimen removal.

The surgeon uses coagulation to cauterize the resection margin of the liver, preventing any oozing from the cut surface. The entire liver surface is covered, and a hemostatic agent is occasionally applied to ensure hemostasis.
At the end, total blood loss was less than one hundred cc's and the specimen was removed through a port site or little opening just at the patient's umbilicus or belly button.

After a very brief hospital stay, the patient was discharged home and reported that after about five days, she was participating in full activities including driving and had really no pain at all. She's able to resume other cancer treatments without any further delay.
